trans_text:
THE PALACES AND TEMPLES OF KULTEPE
According to this report and the plan we can conclude with certainty that in the
center of the palace there was a stone-paved courtyard flanked on three sides by rooms.
In fact the layout of the north wing and its orientation indicate that a central court
existed to its south. This is very important in the context of Anatolian architectural
B. Hrozny, convinced that the palace belonged to the Hittite Empire period and
was burnt in 1200 B.C.14, believed that tablets with Assyrian writing should be looked for
at a lower level. He wrote that he dug down deep in two places of the citadel, one of
them reaching a depth of 8 m. To judge by the state of the deep hole in the citadel in
1948, and by information provided by elderly villagers who had worked in the 1925
excavations, this deep hole was below the courtyard and the stone based rooms of its
south wing. B. Hrozny went down after removing the floorslabs of the courtyard and the
We excavated all the ridges of the citadel, and dug the areas below the haphazard
dumps of B. Hrozny, and thus brought to light: 1. new rooms of the palace; 2. the
monumental fortification wall of the citadel which forms the outer wall of the palace;
and 3. important sections of the lower older palace and of the older fortification wall of
Regardless of the burnt and ruined condition of the palaces and the fact that large
sections were removed in Late Hittite and Hellenistic-Roman times, every research
scholar, has wished to excavate the citadel. This is what most handicapped our work.
First of all E. Chantre, a well known explorer, opened two pits on the west side of the
citadel and here removed some of the rooms as he went down. The illustration in
Mission en Cappadoce, p. 73, fig. 53 (ouverture d'une tranchée dans les parties
centrales du tell) informs us about the method of these excavations. Photographs taken
in the course of B. Hrozny's excavations, now preserved in the archives of the General
Directorate of Monuments and Museums enlighten us on the second phase of citadel
excavations (Pl.3, 1-2; 4,2). E. Chantre, contemplating the fierceness of the
conflagration, explained its crater shape as follows:15 ...sa physionomie en forme de
cratère lui vient sans dout de l'origine volcanique du sol sur lequel il a été élevé.  B.
